subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Memory and Levels of Processing
stimulus:
Dr. Jones was doing a formal research study of the effect of mental imagery on memory. She randomly assigned students in the psychology course she was teaching into two groups and required them to complete the task as part of their coursework. Forty students were in each group. Each group was presented the same list of thirty word pairs (e.g., tree/book, house/street, friend/cat). Group A was told to form a mental image for each pair. Group B was not given that instruction. Each group was given two minutes to memorize the pairs of words. Dr. Jones then tested their recall of the list by giving each group the first word and asking them to remember the second word. Dr. Jones recorded the number of words recalled by each group and then debriefed the participants. The results are presented in the table below.

| | Group A | Group B |
|---|---------|----------|
| Mean Number of Words Recalled | 25 | 10 |
| Standard Deviation | 4 | 6 |

parts.prompt: Identify the independent variable presented in the study.
parts.scoring_notes: Student must identify the IV as the presence or absence of the mental imagery instruction (whether participants were told to form a mental image). Answers that correctly identify the manipulation are acceptable even if phrasing varies. Naming just 'mental imagery' without indicating it was the instructional condition given to Group A but not Group B is acceptable if the meaning is clear.

scoring_points.description: Identifies the independent variable as the instruction to use mental imagery (or the presence/absence of the mental imagery instruction given to participants).

required_elements: Identifies the IV as the mental imagery instruction — whether participants were told to form mental images (Group A) vs. not told to do so (Group B)
alternatives.correct_example: The independent variable is whether participants were instructed to form a mental image for each word pair.

required_elements: Identifies IV as the use of mental imagery / the mental imagery condition
alternatives.correct_example: The independent variable is the use of mental imagery.

wrong_examples: The independent variable is the number of words recalled. (this is the dependent variable)
The independent variable is the word pairs given to participants. (both groups received the same word pairs — this was held constant, not manipulated)
common_traps: Identifying the dependent variable (words recalled) as the independent variable
Identifying the word pairs as the IV — the word pairs were identical in both groups and were not the manipulated factor
Describing the groups (Group A vs. Group B) as the IV rather than identifying what was manipulated between the groups
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify the independent variable as the mental imagery instruction — whether participants were told to form mental images (Group A received the instruction; Group B did not). The IV is the manipulated variable. The word pairs were the same for both groups (a constant, not the IV). The number of words recalled is the dependent variable. Naming the groups themselves (Group A vs. Group B) as the IV does not earn the point — the IV is what differed between the groups.

sample_responses.response: The independent variable is whether participants were instructed to form a mental image for each word pair.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the manipulated factor — the instruction to use (or not use) mental imagery.

sample_responses.response: The independent variable is the use of mental imagery.
sample_responses.commentary: Concise and correct — the presence or absence of the mental imagery strategy is what was manipulated.

sample_responses.response: The independent variable is the number of words recalled.
sample_responses.commentary: Words recalled is the measured outcome — the dependent variable, not the independent variable.

sample_responses.response: The independent variable is the word pairs given to participants.
sample_responses.commentary: Both groups received the same word pairs; the word pairs were held constant and were not manipulated between groups.

parts.scoring_notes: The control group is Group B — the group that did NOT receive the mental imagery instruction. Accept 'Group B' or any description that identifies the group that memorized word pairs without being told to form mental images.

scoring_points.description: Identifies Group B (the group that was NOT told to form mental images) as the control group.

required_elements: Identifies Group B as the control group OR describes the group not given the mental imagery instruction as the control group
alternatives.correct_example: The control group is Group B, because they were not given the instruction to form mental images.

wrong_examples: The control group is Group A. (Group A received the experimental treatment — the mental imagery instruction — so it is the experimental group, not the control)
There is no control group because both groups memorized the same word pairs. (the control/experimental distinction is based on whether the treatment was applied, not on the stimulus materials)
common_traps: Identifying Group A as the control group — Group A received the treatment (mental imagery instruction) and is the experimental group
Confusing control group with the group that had lower performance — control status depends on treatment receipt, not outcome
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify Group B as the control group. Group B is the control group because it did not receive the experimental treatment (the mental imagery instruction). Group A received the instruction and is the experimental group. Control/experimental status is determined by receipt of the treatment, not by performance outcomes. Stating 'there is no control group because both groups received the same word pairs' is incorrect — the same stimulus material does not determine group status.

sample_responses.response: The control group is Group B, because they were not given the instruction to form mental images.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies Group B as the control group and gives the correct reason — they did not receive the experimental treatment (mental imagery instruction).

sample_responses.response: The control group is Group A.
sample_responses.commentary: Group A received the mental imagery instruction — that is the experimental treatment. Group A is the experimental group, not the control group.

sample_responses.response: There is no control group because both groups memorized the same list of word pairs.
sample_responses.commentary: The same stimulus material being used in both groups does not mean there is no control group. The distinction is whether the group received the manipulation (mental imagery instruction) or not.

parts.prompt: Explain why the type of research design being used is appropriate for this study.
parts.scoring_notes: The design is an experiment (specifically a true experiment with random assignment). Student must (1) identify it as an experiment or describe random assignment as a feature, and (2) explain WHY that design is appropriate — specifically that it allows cause-and-effect conclusions about mental imagery and memory recall.

scoring_points.description: Explains that the experimental design with random assignment is appropriate because it allows Dr. Jones to establish a cause-and-effect relationship between mental imagery use and memory recall by controlling for individual differences and confounding variables.

required_elements: Identifies the design as an experiment (or notes random assignment was used)
Explains that this design allows cause-and-effect conclusions OR that random assignment controls for confounding variables/individual differences
alternatives.correct_example: The experiment is appropriate because random assignment ensures the groups are equivalent at the start, so any difference in recall can be attributed to the mental imagery instruction rather than pre-existing differences between participants.

required_elements: Notes that random assignment / experimental design establishes causation (not just correlation)
alternatives.correct_example: An experimental design is appropriate because it allows Dr. Jones to conclude that mental imagery caused the difference in recall, not just that the two are correlated.

wrong_examples: The experimental design is appropriate because it has a large sample size. (sample size affects statistical power but is not what makes the design appropriate for establishing causation)
The design is appropriate because Dr. Jones debriefed participants. (debriefing is an ethical requirement, not a design justification)
common_traps: Explaining the design is appropriate because of the sample size or the data table, rather than focusing on random assignment and causal inference
Identifying the design as correlational — Dr. Jones used random assignment, making it a true experiment
Describing WHY random assignment was used (e.g., 'to be fair') without connecting it to controlling confounds or establishing causation
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must (1) identify the design as an experiment or note that random assignment was used, and (2) explain why this makes the design appropriate — specifically that it allows cause-and-effect conclusions (Dr. Jones can attribute any memory difference to the imagery instruction, not to pre-existing participant differences). Large sample size is a statistical power consideration, not a causal reasoning justification. Debriefing is an ethical requirement, not a design feature. The design is NOT correlational — random assignment makes it a true experiment.

sample_responses.response: The experiment is appropriate because random assignment ensures the groups are equivalent at the start, so any difference in recall can be attributed to the mental imagery instruction rather than pre-existing differences between participants.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies random assignment as the key feature and explains that it allows causal inference by controlling for pre-existing differences.

sample_responses.response: An experimental design is appropriate because it allows Dr. Jones to conclude that mental imagery caused the difference in recall, not just that the two are correlated.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly explains that the experiment allows causal conclusions rather than just observing a correlation.

sample_responses.response: The experimental design is appropriate because the study has a large sample size of 80 students.
sample_responses.commentary: Sample size affects statistical power, not the appropriateness of the design for establishing causation. The key justification is random assignment enabling causal inference.

parts.prompt: Explain what the different standard deviations indicate about the data from the two groups.
parts.scoring_notes: Group A: SD = 4 (smaller spread). Group B: SD = 6 (larger spread). Student must explain what the difference in SD values indicates about variability/consistency — Group A's scores are more tightly clustered around the mean; Group B's scores are more spread out / more variable. Stating which group had more variability and why this matters is required for full credit.

scoring_points.description: Explains that Group A's smaller standard deviation (4) indicates its scores were more consistent/clustered closely around the mean of 25, while Group B's larger standard deviation (6) indicates greater variability/spread in scores around the mean of 10 — meaning participants in Group B varied more widely in how many words they recalled.

required_elements: Identifies that Group A had less variability (scores more consistent/clustered) than Group B
Identifies that Group B had more variability (scores more spread out) than Group A
alternatives.correct_example: Group A's standard deviation of 4 indicates that participants' scores were relatively consistent, clustering close to the mean of 25. Group B's standard deviation of 6 indicates that its scores were more variable — individual participants recalled very different numbers of words, with scores spread further from the mean of 10.

wrong_examples: Group B's higher standard deviation means Group B did better than Group A. (SD indicates variability, not performance level — Group A had higher recall despite lower SD)
The different standard deviations mean the groups were not equivalent. (SD reflects within-group variability, not between-group comparability at the start of the study)
common_traps: Confusing standard deviation with the mean — a higher SD does not indicate better performance
Saying Group B's higher SD means its results are less accurate or less reliable rather than simply more variable
Only describing one group's SD without comparing the two
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain what the different standard deviation values indicate about the data — specifically that Group A's smaller SD (4) indicates its scores were more consistent and clustered around the mean, while Group B's larger SD (6) indicates greater variability/spread in scores. Standard deviation reflects within-group variability, not performance level and not between-group equivalence. A higher SD does not mean better performance.

sample_responses.response: Group A's standard deviation of 4 indicates that participants' scores were relatively consistent, clustering close to the mean of 25. Group B's standard deviation of 6 indicates that its scores were more variable — individual participants recalled very different numbers of words, with scores spread further from the mean of 10.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly interprets both SD values in terms of within-group variability and makes a comparison between the two groups.

sample_responses.response: Group B's higher standard deviation means Group B performed better than Group A.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses standard deviation with the mean. A higher SD indicates more variability, not higher performance. Group A had a higher mean recall (25 vs. 10) despite having the lower SD.

sample_responses.response: The different standard deviations mean the groups were not equivalent at the start of the study.
sample_responses.commentary: SD measures within-group variability around each group's mean, not the comparability of the groups to each other at the outset. Random assignment is what establishes equivalence between groups.

parts.prompt: Explain the ethical flaw that is explicitly presented in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: The ethical flaw is that Dr. Jones required students to participate as part of their coursework — participation was mandatory, not voluntary. This violates the APA ethical guideline of voluntary participation / informed consent (students could not freely decline). The scenario explicitly states she 'required them to complete the task as part of their coursework.' The student must identify this coercion/lack of voluntary participation specifically.

scoring_points.description: Identifies the ethical flaw as the lack of voluntary participation: Dr. Jones required students in her course to participate as part of their coursework, meaning they could not freely decline without academic consequence — violating the principle that participation in research must be voluntary and free from coercion.

required_elements: Identifies that requiring students to participate as part of their coursework constitutes coercion or violation of voluntary participation
Connects this to the APA ethical standard of voluntary participation or freedom from coercion
alternatives.correct_example: The ethical flaw is that Dr. Jones required participation as part of the students' coursework, meaning they could not opt out without potential academic penalty. This violates the APA principle of voluntary participation because participants were coerced into joining the study.

required_elements: States that students were required/forced to participate (not given free choice to decline)
alternatives.correct_example: The ethical flaw is that students were required to participate — their participation was mandatory, not voluntary, which violates ethical guidelines for research with human participants.

wrong_examples: The ethical flaw is that Dr. Jones did not debrief participants. (the scenario explicitly states she did debrief them — this is incorrect)

The ethical flaw is that Dr. Jones used her own students. (using students from one's own class is a dual-relationship concern, but the explicit flaw stated in the scenario is the mandatory participation requirement)
common_traps: Claiming there was no debriefing — the scenario clearly states participants were debriefed after the study
Identifying the correct person (Dr. Jones using her own students) as the flaw but missing the specific stated problem of mandatory participation
Describing a general concern about ethics without identifying the specific explicitly-stated flaw: coerced participation
scoring_points.official_rubric: The explicit ethical flaw in the scenario is that Dr. Jones 'required' students to participate 'as part of their coursework,' violating the principle of voluntary participation. Participants must be free to decline without penalty. The scenario explicitly states debriefing occurred — claiming there was no debriefing is factually incorrect and does not earn the point. The flaw must be the specific mandatory participation issue, not a general ethical concern.

sample_responses.response: The ethical flaw is that Dr. Jones required participation as part of the students' coursework, meaning they could not opt out without potential academic penalty. This violates the APA principle of voluntary participation because participants were coerced into joining the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the specific explicitly-stated flaw (mandatory participation) and correctly labels it as a violation of voluntary participation.

sample_responses.response: The ethical flaw is that students were required to participate — their participation was mandatory, not voluntary.
sample_responses.commentary: Concise identification of the ethical violation: mandatory (not voluntary) participation.

sample_responses.response: The ethical flaw is that Dr. Jones did not debrief participants after the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Factually incorrect — the scenario explicitly states that Dr. Jones debriefed participants after the study.

parts.prompt: Explain how the primacy effect could apply to this research.
parts.scoring_notes: Student must (1) define the primacy effect as the tendency to better remember items presented at the beginning of a list (due to transfer into long-term memory via rehearsal), and (2) apply this to the word-pair recall study. The connection to both groups is acceptable, or a connection specifically to one group.

scoring_points.description: Explains that the primacy effect is the tendency to recall items from the beginning of a list better than those in the middle, because the first items have more time to be rehearsed and encoded into long-term memory, and applies this to Dr. Jones's study by noting that participants in both groups would likely recall the first word pairs on the list better than those presented later.

required_elements: Defines primacy effect as better recall for items at the beginning of a list
Applies this to the word-pair recall study: participants would recall the first word pairs better than middle/later ones
alternatives.correct_example: The primacy effect predicts that participants remember the first items on a list better because those items had more time for rehearsal and transfer into long-term memory. In Dr. Jones's study, participants in both groups would likely recall the first word pairs on the list more accurately than those in the middle or at the end.

wrong_examples: The primacy effect means participants remember the last word pairs best. (this describes the recency effect, not the primacy effect)

The primacy effect means Group A remembers more because they used imagery first. (confuses primacy effect with the mental imagery manipulation — primacy is about serial position, not encoding strategy)
common_traps: Confusing the primacy effect with the recency effect — primacy = beginning of the list; recency = end of the list
Applying primacy to the beginning of the study session rather than to the serial position of items within the word list
Describing the primacy effect correctly but failing to connect it to the specific word-pair recall task in the study
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must (1) define the primacy effect as the tendency to better recall items from the beginning of a list (because early items have more time for rehearsal and transfer to long-term memory), and (2) apply this to Dr. Jones's word-pair study — participants would recall the first word pairs more accurately than those in the middle or end. Describing the recency effect (better recall for items at the end) does not earn the point. Applying primacy to the start of the study session rather than to item serial position within the list does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The primacy effect predicts that participants would remember the first word pairs on the list better because those items had more time for rehearsal and transfer into long-term memory. In Dr. Jones's study, both groups would likely recall the first several word pairs more accurately than those in the middle or at the end.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines primacy effect (better recall for early list items due to rehearsal time) and applies it directly to the word-pair recall task.

sample_responses.response: The primacy effect means that participants in Dr. Jones's study would remember the last word pairs on the list best because they were most recently seen.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the recency effect (better recall for items at the end of the list), not the primacy effect (better recall for items at the beginning).

sample_responses.response: The primacy effect means Group A remembered more because they were using imagery from the beginning of the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses the primacy effect (serial position memory advantage for early items) with the mental imagery manipulation. Primacy is about position in the word list, not about encoding strategy.

parts.prompt: Explain how levels of processing are related to this research.
parts.scoring_notes: Student must (1) define levels of processing as the theory (Craik and Lockhart) that deeper/more elaborate encoding leads to better memory retention than shallow processing, and (2) connect this to the study — Group A's mental imagery instruction prompted deeper (elaborative/semantic) processing of the word pairs, resulting in better recall compared to Group B's shallower processing.

scoring_points.description: Explains that according to the levels of processing framework, information processed more deeply (semantically or elaboratively) is retained better than information processed at a shallow level (e.g., structural or phonemic). Applies this to the study by explaining that Group A's mental imagery instruction encouraged deeper, more elaborative encoding of the word pairs, which is why Group A recalled significantly more words (mean 25) than Group B (mean 10), who used shallower processing.

required_elements: Defines levels of processing as the idea that deeper/more elaborate encoding leads to better memory than shallow processing
Applies this to the study: Group A's imagery instructions produced deeper encoding, explaining their higher recall compared to Group B
alternatives.correct_example: Levels of processing theory states that information encoded at a deeper, more meaningful level is remembered better than shallowly processed information. In this study, Group A's instruction to form mental images encouraged deeper elaborative encoding of the word pairs, which led to their higher mean recall of 25 words compared to Group B's mean of 10, who processed the pairs more shallowly without the imagery strategy.

required_elements: Identifies that imagery = deep processing and no imagery = shallow processing
Links deeper processing to better recall outcomes in the study
alternatives.correct_example: Levels of processing predicts that the more deeply information is processed, the better it will be remembered. Group A used mental imagery, which is a form of deep/elaborative processing, while Group B processed the word pairs at a shallower level — consistent with Group A's much higher recall score.

wrong_examples: Levels of processing means that Group A practiced more than Group B. (levels of processing refers to depth/type of encoding, not amount of practice or repetition)

Levels of processing shows that Group B should have recalled more because they had to work harder without imagery. (inverts the theory — deeper, more elaborate processing predicts better recall, not effortful shallow processing)
common_traps: Confusing levels of processing with the amount of rehearsal (maintenance vs. elaborative rehearsal distinction is related but distinct from the levels framework)
Describing levels of processing only theoretically without applying it to the specific comparison between Group A and Group B in the study
Suggesting shallow processing leads to better memory — the theory predicts the opposite
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must (1) define levels of processing as the principle that deeper, more elaborative encoding produces better memory retention than shallow encoding, and (2) apply this to the study by explaining that Group A's mental imagery instruction produced deeper encoding than Group B's unguided memorization, accounting for Group A's higher recall (mean 25 vs. 10). Levels of processing is about encoding depth/type, not amount of practice. Shallow processing does not lead to better memory — the theory predicts the opposite.

sample_responses.response: Levels of processing theory states that information encoded at a deeper, more meaningful level is remembered better than shallowly processed information. Group A's instruction to form mental images encouraged deeper elaborative encoding of the word pairs, which explains their higher mean recall of 25 words compared to Group B's mean of 10.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines levels of processing (deeper encoding = better memory) and applies it to both groups, explaining the recall difference.

sample_responses.response: Group A used mental imagery, which is a form of deep/elaborative processing, while Group B processed the word pairs at a shallower level — consistent with Group A's much higher recall score.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies levels of processing by contrasting deep processing (imagery in Group A) with shallow processing (Group B), linking to the recall outcome.

sample_responses.response: Levels of processing means Group A practiced more than Group B.
sample_responses.commentary: Levels of processing is about the depth/type of encoding, not amount of practice or repetition. More practice is not the same as deeper processing.
